narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put thought into pursuing this path, but the admissions model has flagged concerns about your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. It predicted a 66.6% chance that you would fail, which is high enough that the university automatically rejected your application to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. The model's decision hinged mostly on your academic performance in school, particularly your grades in later years, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction. Your Year 3 decile ranking of 1—well below the typical student's 5.5—signaled to the model that you struggled relative to peers, strongly influencing its pessimistic view despite your full-time commitment, which is common among applicants. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed toward the failure prediction, though less forcefully, as it was another early sign of being in the bottom tier compared to the average of about 5.7. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually pulled in the opposite direction, nudging the prediction toward success since it's close to the typical 3.21 average and showed some academic resilience. Overall, these school deciles outweighed your other strengths, like your family income level, LSAT score of 26.5, or demographic background, in the model's eyes. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your lower decile rankings put you in a riskier group where first-time failure rates are higher. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a low-income background—many overcome similar starts—but the model focuses on patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above the 36.7 average, or strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve those decile rankings. You could also target schools with more holistic reviews or programs supporting full-time students from varied backgrounds. With targeted improvements, reapplying next cycle could shift the odds in your favor. Keep pushing; your story isn't over yet.
